name: Xenogeneic mitochondrial transplantation improved selected mouse age-associated phenotypes
summary: A 2026 mouse study broadens the direct animal-aging support map for mitochondrial delivery, while adding source-compatibility and mitochondrial-quality caveats.
statement: In mice, xenogeneic mitochondrial transplantation improved selected age-associated phenotypes and mitochondrial functional readouts while engaging host mitochondrial quality-control-related pathways.
population_or_model: Mice receiving xenogeneic mitochondrial transplantation, with detailed evidence reported for yak-derived donor mitochondria
time_horizon: Short-term preclinical phenotype and mitochondrial-function assessment
quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports that efficacy declined progressively with room-temperature damage to donor mitochondria and was largely lost after complete disruption.
caveats: This is animal evidence and does not establish treated-human aging efficacy.
The most detailed evidence used yak-derived mitochondria, so broader donor equivalence remains unresolved.
Long-term safety, germline relevance, immune compatibility, and pathway-specific dependence remain undefined.
The abstract reports selected age-associated phenotypes rather than lifespan or durable multimorbidity outcomes.
